name: Bone Tools
tagline: Worked skeletal elements for needles, awls, points, and soft hammers
problem:
Stone flakes cut and scrape, but many tasks need slender stiffness: piercing hide without huge tears, guiding cord through mesh, picking apart fibres, or spreading pressure in pressure flaking. Wood can work but dulls and splits across grain. Bone and antler occupy a middle hardness: tougher than wood along long axes, easier to shape than stone in elongate forms, and available wherever large animals are processed.

The problem bone solves is manufacturability of slender tools before metal wire drawing. A bone needle enables sewn clothing — a thermal technology multiplier. An awl enables consistent holes for lashings and later rivets conceptually.

Bone is also information-dense waste: if you already process animals for meat, converting metapodials and ribs into tools extracts value from refuse.
overview:
Bone tools are made by sawing, grooving, snapping, and grinding. Long bones yield splinters with natural stiffness; metapodials yield rods; ribs yield flat strips. Splintering follows collagen-apatite composite structure — sharp stone saws score a groove, then bending snaps along the weak plane. Points are shaped by abrasion on sandstone or fine-grained stone; polishing reduces friction when piercing.

Needles require an eye — drilled with a stone point in a bow drill or pushed through with gradual enlargement. Eyes weaken cross-section; design balances length and thickness so bending does not snap the web.

Antler differs: it is remodelled bone grown as weapon and tool stock. Tines detach or are sectioned; antler billet is the classic soft hammer for knapping. Dense antler resists shock and spreads impact.

Preservation bias makes bone tools look rarer than they were — many contexts destroy organic artefacts. Where preserved, bone harpoons, spear straighteners, and weaving tools show deep integration with fishing and textiles.

principles.name: Composite structure
principles.explanation: Bone combines flexible collagen and hard hydroxyapatite; fracture paths follow porosity and growth zones — tool makers learn to read grain.

principles.name: Work hardening by polishing
principles.explanation: Abrasion compresses surface fibres and reduces snag — critical for needles passing through tight holes.

principles.name: Stress concentration at eyes
principles.explanation: Holes create notch stress; rounded eyes and generous material around the web reduce catastrophic snaps.

principles.name: Orthotropic strength
principles.explanation: Bone is stronger along the long axis of the shaft — align tools with loaded direction.

components.name: Bone blank
components.function: Starting stock sized for intended tool class
components.position: Split from long bone or cut from antler beam
components.madeFrom: Long bones of medium-large mammals or antler
components.criticalNote: Fresh green bone cuts easier; dry bone is brittle — soak if needed

components.name: Working tip
components.function: Concentrates force for piercing or scoring
components.madeFrom: Same bone; sometimes bevelled on two planes for awl geometry

components.name: Needle eye (if applicable)
components.position: Near midpoint or proximal end depending on tradition
components.madeFrom: Drilled hole, sometimes ground slot eye in antiquity
components.criticalNote: Deburr interior — thread abrasion fails sewing projects silently

components.name: Grip zone
components.function: Comfort and torque transmission for awls and chisels
components.position: Proximal end; sometimes wrapped with cordage
components.madeFrom: Unmodified bone surface or wrapped fibre

rawMaterials.name: Long bones and antler
rawMaterials.purpose: Rigid blanks for points and billets
earthLocations: Archaeological and modern butchery sites worldwide
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Any cultured animal agriculture; synthetic polymers replace function when available
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Deflesh, degrease optionally by boiling, dry slowly to reduce cracking

rawMaterials.name: Abrasive stone
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Diamond abrasive later; early — silica-rich regolith pressed into blocks
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Flatten working surface by grinding mutually

rawMaterials.name: Drill and bow-drill hearth
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Same pattern with imported metals or electric tools
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Maintain sharp drill tip; clear swarf to reduce heat

buildSteps.title: Select bone element
buildSteps.description: For needles, choose bird long bones or mammal metapodials with straight shafts. For awls, use splinters from long bones. For soft hammers, use antler crown or dense beam sections.
buildSteps.warningNote: Zoonoses from fresh bone handling — wash hands; heat-treat if culturally appropriate

buildSteps.title: Rough out by grooving and snapping
buildSteps.description: Score a deep groove around the shaft, then flex-break. Refine with coarse abrasion to remove splinters.
buildSteps.warningNote: Bone shards are sharp — eye protection

buildSteps.title: Grind point geometry
buildSteps.description: Work tip to a gradual taper for needles; steeper for awls. Check symmetry by rolling on flat stone.
buildSteps.warningNote: Overheating from dry grinding can crack bone — use water

buildSteps.title: Drill eye (needles)
buildSteps.description: Start dimple with graver, drill with bow drill using stone bit, enlarge with tapered bone pin. Polish eye rim.
buildSteps.warningNote: Thin web breaks last — stop enlarging early and finish by pulling abrasive cord through

buildSteps.title: Polish and temper use
buildSteps.description: Final polish with fine grit or leather. Test on scrap hide — smooth draw indicates success.
buildSteps.warningNote: Needles snap if used as levers — bend radius matters

history.event: Eyed needles appear with evidence for fitted clothing and complex textiles.
Barbed bone harpoons and fish gorge hooks proliferate along waterways.
Bone continues alongside ground stone and copper as everyday toolkit elements.

impact:
Bone tools quietly enabled clothing, containers, fishing, and fine crafts. They occupy a durability niche between wood and stone and teach composite material thinking.

Historically, bone and antler remained important into metal ages for pins, combs, and specialised awls. For civilisation-restart scenarios, bone is a bridge material when metal is absent but megafauna or fish skeletons exist.

Ethically and legally, modern makers must respect wildlife protection laws and cultural heritage — the Codex describes principles, not permission to loot sites.
